old_claim.note: Phonograph provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Commercial recorded-music production required phonograph recording/playback and cylinder-record manufacture; the Library of Congress traces the 1887-1888 commercial phonograph platform into 1889 entertainment cylinder production.

source_shape.source_locator: The LOC history describes the Edison Phonograph Company, the 1888 Improved and Perfected Phonographs, musical cylinders for coin-slot phonographs, and later commercial cylinder issue for home entertainment.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source shows commercial recorded-music products emerging through phonograph machines and cylinder recordings.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the target node is commercial sound recording and playback, the phonograph is the direct recording/playback platform rather than merely adjacent enabling context.

ontology_before: Recorded Music Industry treated the phonograph as a generic enabling capability.
ontology_after: Recorded Music Industry treats the phonograph as the required platform for early commercial sound recording and playback.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: phonograph is now required rather than a generic enabling edge.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to music publishing, artist management, or copyright administration without recorded-sound media.
A stronger source showed the first recorded-music industry could operate without phonograph-style sound recording and playback.
The phonograph node were narrowed to exclude cylinder recording/playback machines.
short_reason: The phonograph is the direct platform for early commercial recorded music.
